summary: Jeff Atwood investigates Dave Shea's prediction that 200 DPI displays will become standard 'in a few years,' examining the historical progression of display resolution from 1984 to 2007. His research reveals that display DPI has only improved by a factor of 1.6 over twenty years, from around 60-72 DPI to roughly 100 DPI. While high-resolution displays exist, they remain prohibitively expensive ($8,000-$20,000) and unavailable through mainstream consumer channels. The only place higher pixel densities appear is in laptops, where physical size constraints force manufacturers to pack more pixels into fixed screen sizes. Atwood concludes that reaching 200 DPI standard displays is unlikely even within ten years, contradicting the optimistic predictions of display technology advancement.
key_points: Display resolution has improved by less than a factor of two in twenty years, making it one of the slowest-advancing computer hardware categories
High-resolution displays (200 DPI) existed in 2007 but cost $8,000-$20,000, placing them far outside consumer reach
Consumer behavior favoring 'bigger is better' works against pixel density improvements, as manufacturers prioritize larger screens over higher DPI
Laptops are the only segment showing meaningful DPI increases because the fixed form factor prevents screens from getting physically larger
Even at 147 DPI (the highest laptop option available), displays were only halfway to the 200 DPI target
money_quotes: The resolution of computer displays has increased by less than a factor of two over the last twenty years.
I can't think of any other piece of computer hardware that has improved so little since 1984.
At consumer prices and through consumer outlets, high resolution displays simply don't exist.
The notebook form factor physically precludes the display from getting any larger.
If notebook vendors could somehow fit a folding 30" LCD panel into a laptop, they absolutely would.
Short of some kind of miraculous technological breakthrough, I can't see computer displays reaching 200 DPI in 'a few years.'
All the evidence of history – not to mention typical consumer 'bigger is better' behavior – is overwhelming.
key_insight: Consumer market forces favoring larger screens over higher pixel density, combined with the prohibitive cost of high-DPI manufacturing, have caused display resolution to stagnate as one of the slowest-improving areas in computer hardware.
